mnemonics: SAFER: Signal Action Follows These Essential Rules - A(tomic variables), S(afe functions only), F(lag setting), E(errno handling), R(eentrant code only)
ASAF: Async-Signal-Avoid Functions - Remember which functions are NOT safe to call in handlers
VSTAR: Volatile Sig_atomic_T And atomic-pseudo-mnemonic: Always Remember to combine both qualifiers when sharing variables

acronyms.expansion: EINTRupted - Error returned when system call is interrupted by signal
acronyms.usage: When read()/write() returns -1, check if errno==EINTR and restart the call

acronyms.expansion: Signal Action RESTART - Flag to automatically restart interrupted system calls
acronyms.usage: Use in struct sigaction: act.sa_flags = SA_RESTART;

acronyms.expansion: SIGnal ATOMIC TYPE - Type for atomic operations in signal context
acronyms.usage: volatile sig_atomic_t flag; // for signal-safe communication

visualTips.visualization: Draw a timeline: [Main Program] --signal arrives--> [Handler executes quickly, sets flag] --> [Handler returns] --> [Main program checks flag and does work] --> Emphasize minimal work in handler box
Create a two-column table: LEFT = SAFE (_exit, signal, write, fflush) | RIGHT = UNSAFE (printf, malloc, fprintf, fopen). Draw a wall between them representing the signal safety barrier
Draw overlapping circles: one labeled 'volatile' (prevents register caching), one labeled 'sig_atomic_t' (ensures atomic read/write). The intersection = the correct declaration: volatile sig_atomic_t

analogies.analogy: Think of a signal handler like an emergency responder arriving at an accident scene. They shouldn't try to do complex repairs (like malloc, complex I/O); they should only stabilize the patient (set a flag) and call for help (let main program do the work).

This is like a person on a walkie-talkie in a war zone - they need both a clear channel (atomic to not be interrupted) and to speak actual words not stored thoughts (volatile to not be optimized away).

It's like having someone tap you on the shoulder (signal) but you're wearing noise-canceling headphones. The tap also turns on a light switch (pipe write). You see the light and know to check - converting silent tap into visible notification.
